task_prompt:
Answer the following multiple-choice question. You may reason briefly, but end your response with your choice on its own line in the exact form 'Answer: X', where X is one of the option letters.

# Question
A 19-year-old man who plays college lacrosse presents with a one-week history of malaise, headache, fever, and sore throat that have made it impossible for him to participate in lacrosse practice.


He has a fever of 38.9°C, pharyngeal erythema and exudates, and tender posterior cervical adenopathy. His spleen is not palpable.


He is up to date with Covid-19 vaccinations, and testing for SARS-CoV-2 is negative. A heterophile antibody latex agglutination and Epstein-Barr virus viral capsid antigen immunoglobulin M and immunoglobulin G are positive.


Which one of the following criteria best determines when this athlete is likely to be able to safely return to full activity, including sports, assuming resolution of symptoms?

# Options
A) Normalization of liver function tests
B) Resolution of cervical lymphadenopathy
C) Three weeks elapse after the onset of symptoms
D) Seven weeks elapse after the onset of symptoms
E) Two consecutive negative heterophile antibody tests
F) Return of normal exercise tolerance
G) An abdominal ultrasound confirms the absence of splenomegaly
H) Negative EBV IgM antibodies
I) EBV viral load becomes undetectable
J) Complete resolution of fatigue symptoms
prompt:
Answer the following multiple-choice question. You may reason briefly, but end your response with your choice on its own line in the exact form 'Answer: X', where X is one of the option letters.

# Question
A 19-year-old man who plays college lacrosse presents with a one-week history of malaise, headache, fever, and sore throat that have made it impossible for him to participate in lacrosse practice.


He has a fever of 38.9°C, pharyngeal erythema and exudates, and tender posterior cervical adenopathy. His spleen is not palpable.


He is up to date with Covid-19 vaccinations, and testing for SARS-CoV-2 is negative. A heterophile antibody latex agglutination and Epstein-Barr virus viral capsid antigen immunoglobulin M and immunoglobulin G are positive.


Which one of the following criteria best determines when this athlete is likely to be able to safely return to full activity, including sports, assuming resolution of symptoms?

# Options
A) Normalization of liver function tests
B) Resolution of cervical lymphadenopathy
C) Three weeks elapse after the onset of symptoms
D) Seven weeks elapse after the onset of symptoms
E) Two consecutive negative heterophile antibody tests
F) Return of normal exercise tolerance
G) An abdominal ultrasound confirms the absence of splenomegaly
H) Negative EBV IgM antibodies
I) EBV viral load becomes undetectable
J) Complete resolution of fatigue symptoms
output:
The question is about infectious mononucleosis (EBV) and return to sports.

The key concern for return to sports in mono is splenic rupture risk. The spleen enlarges in most cases of mono (even if not palpable on exam), and splenic rupture is the most dangerous complication.

Current guidelines (including from the American Medical Society for Sports Medicine) recommend:
- No contact sports for at least 3 weeks from symptom onset (minimum)
- After 3 weeks, if the patient is afebrile, asymptomatic, and splenomegaly has resolved (confirmed by ultrasound), they can return to non-contact sports
- For contact/collision sports (lacrosse is a contact sport), ultrasound confirmation of no splenomegaly is recommended before return

The question asks what "best determines" when the athlete can safely return to full activity including sports. The spleen may not be palpable on exam yet still be enlarged - ultrasound is more reliable. The standard of care is to use abdominal ultrasound to confirm absence of splenomegaly before return to contact sports.

Option C (3 weeks) is the minimum time frame, but time alone isn't sufficient - clinical assessment and ideally imaging are needed.

Option G (abdominal ultrasound confirms absence of splenomegaly) is the best answer because:
1. Lacrosse is a contact sport
2. The spleen may be enlarged even if not palpable
3. Ultrasound provides objective confirmation that the spleen has returned to normal size

Answer: G

result.raw_eval_output: You answered G. That is incorrect.
